problem_summary: 2019 MCM Problem B "Go Safe with Drones" (discrete optimization): design a DroneGo disaster-relief fleet for hurricane-struck Puerto Rico. The team had to (1) choose a fleet from 7 drone types (A-G) + tethered H, (2) pack up to 3 ISO cargo containers with 3 medical-package types, (3) locate those containers optimally, and (4) produce delivery routes, schedules, and flight plans that BOTH deliver medical supplies fast AND maximize video reconnaissance of damaged roads. This team (control 1923964) decomposed it into a delivery model, a reconnaissance model, and a combined multi-objective model, applying their solution to Puerto Rico and validating on Haiti's 2010 earthquake.
models_used: Delivery model as combinatorial facility-location: minimize total flight time. Uses a 0-1 Destinations Combination Matrix B (2^n-1 rows) and a Drones Combination Matrix D (2^m-1 rows), a mapping f from destination-combos to drone-combos, and a distance matrix A.

Optimal Combination Algorithm (OCA) — a custom 4-procedure exhaustive/greedy search: (1) isolate destinations beyond all drones' radius, (2) enumerate disjoint destination groups covering all sites within container budget, (3) greedily assign drones to air-lines per group computing group min-time, (4) compare groups and pick the global minimum.

Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm — run on an interpolated 3-D terrain grid to compute realistic over-the-ground flight distances (not 2-D straight lines) and to derive the actual 3-D delivery routes.

Reconnaissance model as a graph-importance maximization: settlements are nodes, road 'segments' are edges. General importance G = (I + X)S, where S = self-importance vector (city-level score 1-6), X = 0-1 connection matrix, so connective importance C = XS. Segment importance E(ij) = g_i + g_j.

Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) — particles carry (longitude, latitude, velocity); fitness Z = sum of importance of all segments inside each container's detectable disk (radius r + camera scan radius R). Used to find best container locations maximizing reconnoitered road value. Cross-checked against a brute-force grid exhaustion.

Combined multi-objective model: weighted combination of delivery-time objective and reconnaissance-value objective; weights set per scenario. For Puerto Rico they argue supply >> demand and delivery times are near-uniform, so they collapse it to maximizing reconnaissance value within the delivery-feasible region.

Supporting sub-models: negative-exponential fit T = T0·e^(-λb) for flight time vs payload (fitted to 3 battery curves, r≈-0.998), spline interpolation of λ across drone types, and a camera-coverage geometry model FOV/scan-radius R = H·tan(α+θ/2) from sensor-resolution constraints (gave R = 2.75 km, α = 51.5°).
key_techniques: Problem reduction by transforming road-importance evaluation into settlement-importance evaluation — the team's self-declared 'most creative work'. Instead of scoring every road segment directly, segment value = sum of its two endpoint settlements' general importance, collapsing edge-evaluation into node-evaluation.

Linear-algebra encoding of a graph: G = (I+X)S compactly captures both a node's own importance and its neighbors' importance via the adjacency/connection matrix, making the importance field a single matrix-vector product.

Realism injection at the 'model preparation' stage: 3-D topographic reconstruction from 110+ sampled elevation points + interpolation, payload-dependent navigation radius (worst-case full-cargo flight time for safety), and explicit camera scanning geometry — these turned an abstract problem into a physically grounded one before any optimization.

Choosing two complementary solvers for the two location problems: deterministic Dijkstra for delivery routing/distance, metaheuristic PSO for continuous location search over a non-trivial fitness landscape, then validating PSO with an independent grid-exhaustion.

Scenario-driven multi-objective collapse: rather than hand-wave Pareto fronts, they quantitatively argued (supply covers ~2 years, delivery times near-uniform across feasible region) that one objective dominates, reducing the combined problem to a single tractable maximization.

Generalization test on a genuinely different, larger case (Haiti, ~3x Puerto Rico) rather than a toy perturbation, with predicted container locations overlapping real 2010 relief centers — strong external validity evidence.
Greedy heuristic inside OCA for drone-to-airline assignment, with a special-case closed-form (flights_needed × max_flight_time) for single-destination combos.
why_it_won: "From a judge's lens this paper wins on practicability and completeness, not mathematical fireworks. (1) It actually delivers every deliverable the prompt asks for — concrete fleet (drone B and F), explicit container packing (e.g. Container 1: 1908 MED1, 954 MED2, 954 MED3), numeric lat/long locations, ordered flight plans (segment-by-segment accessing sequence from center outward), and a schedule — judges reward teams that close the loop on the CEO's actual questions. (2) The reconnaissance-importance idea (transform road value into settlement value via G=(I+X)S) is a genuinely elegant, defensible modeling move that simplifies an otherwise messy edge-evaluation problem; judges remember one clever idea. (3) Heavy realism (3-D terrain + Dijkstra over-ground distance, payload-vs-radius, camera FOV geometry) signals engineering maturity. (4) It validates externally on Haiti and runs real sensitivity analyses (linear vs exponential importance scoring; navigation radius 23/26/40 km), showing the result is robust and not cherry-picked. (5) The summary sheet and CEO memo are crisp, decision-oriented, and translate math into operational recommendations — exactly the communication judges score highly."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet names the methods (Dijkstra + PSO), the three nested models, and the headline results up front. The CEO memo is a standout: it maps each business scenario (medical urgency / reconstruction urgency / both) to which model to use, states the creative settlement-importance idea in plain language, and gives concrete numeric deliverables plus two caveats (rank settlements correctly; calibrate real navigation radius). 'Model Preparations' (Sec 4) is a strong structural choice — it isolates all the physics/data-fitting (terrain, payload-time, camera) before the optimization so the models read cleanly. Each model follows a disciplined Analysis -> Design -> Solve arc, with numbered Procedures/Steps for the algorithms making them reproducible. The Notations table, Assumptions-with-Justifications (each assumption cited), and the explicit Definitions (segment, group, correspondence) give it an academic rigor. Sensitivity analysis is split into importance-method, importance-underestimation, and radius, each with before/after location tables. Strengths/Weaknesses is honest (exponential time complexity, subjective importance, no weather)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The delivery OCA enumerates 2^n-1 destination combinations and 2^m-1 drone combinations — explicitly exponential time complexity (the team admits it), so it only works because Puerto Rico has just 5 destinations and 7 drone types. It would not scale.

Settlement self-importance scores (1-6 by 'city level') are entirely subjective and hand-assigned; sensitivity analysis itself shows individual importance changes DO move the optimal locations materially (propagating through X), so the result is only as good as the human ranking.

The multi-objective 'combination' is largely sidestepped for Puerto Rico — they argue one objective dominates and collapse to single-objective. The genuine weighted trade-off between delivery and reconnaissance is asserted but never actually solved across a weight sweep / Pareto front.
Weather and disaster-severity (the two factors most relevant to a hurricane response) are explicitly excluded; the team flags this as future work but it is a real gap for a disaster problem.
Heavy reliance on PSO without reported convergence diagnostics, particle counts, or repeated-run stability; the grid-exhaustion cross-check mitigates this but the PSO is treated as a black box.
Some quantitative claims are loosely justified (e.g. ascending/descending time ignored, battery mass ignored, vegetation height ignored) — reasonable but stacked, and they compound.
The Dijkstra '3-D over-ground distance' is built on only ~110 interpolated elevation points for the whole island, so route realism is coarser than the framing implies.
reusable_patterns: Three-layer model architecture: solve each single objective in isolation first (delivery-only, recon-only), then 'combine' — a clean narrative that lets judges follow the build-up and is reusable for any multi-objective MCM problem.

Transform-the-evaluation-target trick: when scoring edges (roads, links, routes) is messy, push the value onto nodes and define edge-value = f(endpoint node-values). Encode via G = (I + X)S with a 0-1 adjacency matrix.

Separate a 'Model Preparations' section that converts raw problem data into physically grounded sub-models (terrain interpolation, regression fits like T=T0·e^(-λb), geometry of sensors) BEFORE optimization — keeps the optimization sections clean and signals realism.
Pair a deterministic exact method (Dijkstra) with a metaheuristic (PSO) for continuous facility-location, and validate the metaheuristic against an independent brute-force grid — cheap credibility.
Quantitatively justify collapsing a multi-objective problem to single-objective when one objective is shown to dominate, instead of forcing an arbitrary weighting.
Validate generalization on a second real, larger case (here Haiti) and compare predicted outputs to known ground truth (real relief centers) — far more convincing than synthetic perturbation.

Structure sensitivity analysis around the model's own subjective choices (scoring scheme: linear vs exponential; key physical parameter: navigation radius) and present before/after results in compact tables.
Deliver operational artifacts, not just optima: explicit packing lists, numeric coordinates, and an ordered center-outward flight/accessing sequence — answer the decision-maker's literal questions.
Open with a stakeholder memo that maps scenarios to recommended models and ends with actionable caveats — strong communication template for any 'advise-the-CEO' MCM prompt.
